SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper provides empirical evidence (Pong and Tennis experiments) that eligibility traces help learning and that Adam speeds up training, and it gives a concrete algorithmic description (Alg.1). However, the experimental methodology is limited: only two games are tested, comparisons to published baselines are indirect (no re-runs of competing DQN variants), there is no reporting of variance, seeds, or statistical significance, and figures/tables referenced are not included in the provided text. There are also gaps in discussing off-policy correctness and how Watkins’s Q(λ) is implemented in the forward-view batched replay setting, leaving some technical assumptions unsupported.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Combining eligibility traces with deep recurrent Q-networks and investigating optimizer effects is a reasonable and useful empirical contribution, but it largely builds on existing components (DRQN from Hausknecht & Stone (2015), Watkins Q(λ), and standard optimizers). The paper does not introduce a fundamentally new algorithmic technique; rather it applies and evaluates known ideas together (forward-view traces with sub-trajectory replay and DRQN), so the contribution is incremental rather than highly novel.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized in a standard structure (background, methods, experiments, discussion) and includes a clear algorithm and implementation details (network architecture, λ, trace cutoff, optimizer hyperparameters). However, clarity is weakened by repeated/duplicated paragraphs in the provided text, missing referenced figures/tables, and some under-specified implementation choices (e.g., exact handling of off-policy traces in replay, number of random seeds), which make reproducing and fully understanding the results harder.
